speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about your teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talked about-- you were talking about another opinion that she had about something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she has the-- she said to us that you are sitting there like the Jews in a mikveh. So I said-- I stood up and I said, I don't belong here. And this was not allowed to do it because this was a special place, that you have to stay there. And I said, I can't do what you want to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went again to the director and told him the story. And this is politic. And it was not allowed to belong to organization, even, when you went to high school because school is not a political ground to-- especially to children, you know? And I said, I'm not going back to there, even you're going to dismiss me from the school. He said, don't worry about-- we're going to talk about that. And he-- I didn't go. Would you explain for us what she meant when she said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was-- you have a-- a very heavy rifle in you hands. You couldn't hold it. And then everyone was lying like, you know, really very bad. She can said it in another way. Come-- I know it's very heavy. Come stood up, and I show you how to do it. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But just like that-- she knows that it was two Jewish girls in school and in our class, you know? The-- other than react, I reacted. That's it, you know? Could-- could you tell us the meaning of the word 'mikveh'?

speaker: interviewee
text: The meaning of the word--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mikveh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mikveh is a-- a ritual bath. In a Orthodox shul, they have a-- a-- a-- like a swimming-- a swimming pool, but in a smaller thing. And everyone, woman and man, when he had to have intercourse, he had to go to the mikveh, to this thing, and-- and take a bath. And she also take a bath-- not together, but separate. Man is separate woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it has nothing to do with-- with-- with the teacher. You know? Maybe the rest didn't understand what mikveh means. But, you know, she did. She understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SIGHS] I think I'm not-- I'm-- I'm afraid I'm not understanding what her-- what she was trying to say to you with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she's trying to say that we cannot fight. They had the idea that the Jews cannot fight. When the-- the independent day in-- in May in 1948 in Poland, it was the communist country, communist government. They try-- they helped the Jews in Israel to became a independent country in the beginning, the Russians. They thought that the Jews are going to be communist, and they're going to have another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another country to aggravate the rest of the-- of the world, especially the Americans and the-- and the European free countries. And so-- but the Jews didn't want to. They want to-- they know a lot of the Jews who belonged to [INAUDIBLE] Israel, the-- the parliament there, that they were in Russia, and they were the same thing-- very bad treated there. They-- they-- they didn't say that you Jewish, but they were treated like-- like the worst kind, like-- like Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: If-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when you-- when you graduated high school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after that, what happened then in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in May in 1939. And I was home. And in September 1, I supposed to go to Warsaw, and to be accepted to the dental school. But September 1, they started bombing our city, and I didn't go. I stayed home for the two, three months, how--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you know anything that-- about this, that this was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because a-- a few days-- a few weeks before the invasion of Poland, Chamberlain and-- and Daladier from France-- and Chamberlain from England-- they came back from a convention-- a conference with Hitler. And they had a written statement that they will-- he's not-- he doesn't want a war. He doesn't want anything. And the only thing he wants, he wants Sudetendeutschland, to take a piece of-- of-- piece of Czechoslovakia. And then he's not going anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few weeks later, he start bombing Poland. Then a few weeks after Poland, he goes back to Belgium to-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call? To France, to the little countries there. And they called-- and they took it and run. They invaded the whole Western European countries. And can you tell us what you remember when the bombing started? Where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What you were doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- I was-- I-- I took a bath. And I was in my robe. And they started bombing. My mother was on the market. It was Tuesday-- or Friday. Friday, because every time she goes on, it was Friday. And Mother went to market, you know? And-- and I was prepared [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to wait for mother to make breakfast. And then mother came. And she said, out from the house. And we went to a shelter. And I was in-- only in this robe--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else were you with at home then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my father. My father was the commandant of anti-- what do you call? Anti-- anti-- air-- air-- anti-air-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, they-- they--

speaker: interviewer
text: To fight against the airplanes or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't-- he didn't fight. But he was to tell people where to go, to tell you-- he was in a another block, not in our block. In another block. My brother and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: With us, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the shelters. It was every big house, because we live in a very big house. And they have their own shelters. And there are the-- they are-- the neighbors used to come to our shelter. And there-- there you can see that everyone was very good to each other because it was a tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you do in the shelter when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people were praying. I prayed with them together too. It didn't help, but I prayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a hour. They bomb only the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- not the railroad tracks, because they need it for transportation. They bomb only the chemical factories. They were a little further from the tracks there. And-- and-- and they have a lot of-- of spies in the factory because this was ammunition factory, or it was-- belongs to the Germans, to the French, to our government, too. And, you know, they have those Germans. They-- they know everything what's going on in the factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after you were in the shelter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came home. And we wait for-- for-- my father came home. He demobilized everything. We-- we escaped to a village, to the-- to the peasant who used to bring milk from-- not far from the city. And we stood there a whole week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about the third day of war. It was September 3 until 8. Every night, every second night, we used to go home and bring out the-- what we have in the house, because we left the house in a hurry. We didn't take with us anything else. And so we came to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to this peasant's there. And there, hundreds of thousand soldiers were walking away, back-- and they told us where they-- from where they escaping there. Thousands of soldiers ran through this thing. My father used to go to the peasant and buy milk for those soldiers. They didn't have what to eat. They didn't have no organized-- nothing because the rest left Poland. The big shots left Poland. And they left those soldiers who fight to the last-- to the last drop of-- of blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Poland, yeah. A lot of Jews, a lot of Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- they went to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw them leaving? Or walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them walking away. They didn't know where they were going to be. But we know that they-- they were fighting for about three weeks. And after three weeks, they-- they came over, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what did people tell you about the soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The soldier? They tell that this was soldiers who are heroes. Half of them died. Half of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the-- the fight with the Germans. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And so after the first bombing, you went to the shelter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there about a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I thought--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we stayed there a hour, maybe. And we came home. And then we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go to the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the-- to the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in the village how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: About eight-- eight days. But every night, we came home, or every second night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do at home, when you came home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We take the-- the luggage we left in the house, because people come and they loot it. They came-- one time, we came home and they tried to open our closets. And they were special keys that you cannot open it like that. And he stand up to take the chandelier out of-- and my father came in. And he started screaming that he's going to throw to the balcony out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said he was standing up--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. My father, because he was so enraged. He lost the-- the-- he lost the freedom. And you come to a house and rob.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some from-- villagers. You know? We didn't know. And I begged my father, leave it alone. Leave it alone. It's not worth it. Open the door, throw them out from the house. And so we came every night to take something else. But the chandelier, he wanted to take with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: With our peasant wife who used to bring us milk from her--

speaker: interviewer
text: In their home?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the house. Yeah, in her home. The-- they have-- it was a dirty house because the cow was in the house-- not in the house, but almost in the house. Fleas, the terrible fleas. So we ask her that we want to sleep at the attic. Maybe there, there'll be less-- [LAUGHS] less flies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who slept in the attic, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept in the attic later.

speaker: interviewer
text: We, meaning--

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family. You know? So then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- what did you talk about while you were in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we find--

speaker: interviewer
text: --home?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a-- a-- a old man there who was working by my great-grandfather. They were very rich that time. So they have a lot of children. And he told us how beautiful my great-grandmother was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said they were very rich, do you mean the old man--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or your great-grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Great-- great-grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was an old man staying--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there-- a old peasant--

speaker: interviewer
text: --too?

speaker: interviewee
text: --living there in the house, yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you talked--

speaker: interviewee
text: --not far in the village. And he-- remember, my father used to like to listen to those old people tales or something like that. And he told us he met my mother's side of the family, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this village? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Skarzysko-Koscielny. It means that they have a church there from that-- this was bigger than Skarzysko-Kamienna. But Skarzysko-Kamienna became bigger because they have a lot of factories. They have-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] they have a railroad station in the middle. Everything, who you go from east to west, you have to go to Skarzysko-Kamienna, you see? It was very, very famous about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far from Skarzysko-Kamienna was this other town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- this--

speaker: interviewer
text: About--

speaker: interviewee
text: --village?

speaker: interviewer
text: The village. How far away?

speaker: interviewee
text: 5 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family walk there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we walked. And a funny thing. My sister was four years old that time. And she was holding her nose and said, father, can I leave my nose alone? [LAUGHS] Because, you see, we were-- they teach us, however, it's going to be a gas, because we taught-- they taught that Hitler is throwing gas too, and to have something wet to-- to hold your breath, not to breathe. So she had those things and she hold her nose, my four-year-old sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you left that village?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came home. And then the Germans took over. My father went to the-- the Germans there. And he said that the people are starving already, and to give him permission to bring some food for them. And so he gave him permission to go and to bring the food. They were-- they-- they kill you with kindness. And a few times, they confiscated his-- everything what he bought, and took away from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the date was when the Germans came into your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about a few days later, after we came back home. It was about the 15th of-- of September, [INAUDIBLE]. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you do at that time? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: They organized a health department in the-- in the-- it was not a ghetto yet-- to heal those people. But we didn't have any medication there. Dr. Cahan, the physician, and Doctor-- his wife was a dentist. And she said to my father and mother that I can take Dora as a assistant to her in-- in the office--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and-- and registered us as a maid, like a-- a-- a hired place to be in this health department. So this way, they're not going to touch me, and-- and I'm working fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say they organized a health department. Who do you mean by they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The olders-- the elders, Jewish community. And they-- they organized. They-- they know before maybe what was in Germany, that those-- those-- those health departments were stayed longer, and longer, and longer. You know? And they always thought that this would survive-- the Skarzysko ghetto would survive, and everything was surviving.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to work--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this health department?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your tasks? What kinds of things did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I went and I helped my-- the-- the dentist-- doctor. And she told me what to do. And I-- I helped her a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prepare the-- this was different than now, you know? Technique was completely different. To prep the patient, talk to the patient, to tell her it's going to hurt a little bit, it's not going to hurt a little bit. And so it depends what kind of patient you have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so little by little, I did-- she even asked me to subtract a-- her tooth. I said no. So-- [LAUGHS] you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, when the ghetto was-- you know, after they confiscate everything and after they took away everything from the Jews, the doctor-- Dr. Cahan and her husband came. And he said, you know, that we need about 200 girls to go to war-- to go to war-- to the ammunition factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I need a name, he said to me. And your name would be the nicest thing, because a lot of people will go where you go. You know? And so I said, OK. And I will take you out after the-- the rest go to work. I will take you back, because you work in a health department. So I went. And then they ask-- the-- the boss, a sadist-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what-- his name was Herring.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] was. He was the worst kind of a person. A young man, about 28 years old. Tall, SS. And he asked if-- who speaks German. And some said, Dora, Dora. Who's Dora? And I said in Polish, shut up. I don't want to go. I don't want to be known to you. So he couldn't-- he couldn't say otherwise. So he took me there. And he asked me to write down the names, 200 girls' names. And the girls were cleaning the machines, you know? The ammunition-- You mean that was-- you mean that was their--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --job, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, to clean the thing. And I used to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to put the names in writing. And the next day, Dr. Cahan Came over. And he asked me how was it. I said, thank you for being so nice to me. But I'm not going back. He said, don't go back. The next day, 7 o'clock in the morning, the commandant of the-- of the camp came in. I was in my nightgown, in my robe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came and picked me up like-- I stood like that-- back to the-- to the office there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your robe and nightgown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was sitting there 12 hours, doing nothing. Can you imagine to sit in there doing nothing?

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to make sure I understand how this happened now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the dentist and her husband-- her husband said that you would be a good person to bring other girls? Is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because that way, we can save the ghetto. So to save the ghetto, you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you know what you-- you had no idea? What were you thinking--

speaker: interviewer
text: --at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. I obeyed everyone who was a-- a human being, and they were a human being. This man used to go to the very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] poor people and help them. The doctor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Doctor--

speaker: interviewer
text: --man are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Doctor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Cahan, the-- the physicist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took your name. And-- and you-- did he take you that first day? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- we had to go in the morning, 5 o'clock in the morning, to go and to wait until some Germans would take you--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the first day?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- what--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom-- and whom were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the 200 girls. We walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far?

speaker: interviewee
text: 5-- 5 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the munitions factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The munition factory, yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember that first day when you first saw that commandant or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the-- the commandant? The commandant was very, very nice. He-- but he was crazy about children, and he killed adults. Can you understand those things? He loves children. He took the children. He had a few children in his-- when somebody supposed to come to-- for a visitation in the camp, he took the children to his palace, and he feed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp are you talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Skarzysko-Kamienna. Yeah. And he killed adults. He goes and-- he saw when somebody was sleeping-- not in the barracks. But in the barracks, we have also a lot of everything, what you want-- rats and the bed-- bed bunks, you know? Those fly-- you know, we have bunks three-- three bunks. Not two, but three. And no food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the first day, you were still living at home, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I work-- I worked through the-- same time until '41-'42, until the complete liquidation, because they-- they couldn't-- they didn't have enough transportation to-- put the 7,000 people or 10,000 people. They have to have it a time. They liquidated our ghetto from Rosh Hashanah to Sukkot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what year was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it-- I'm trying to get this in order--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or straighten out. It was-- what is it-- what year was it when you first were taken with the other girls--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to work in the munitions factory?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And do you remember what time of year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about April. Was-- it was cold. So cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- so this-- the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was-- we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to go through a little forest. And one time-- [INAUDIBLE] was the main SS man in this factory-- factories. Three-- it was three factories. And he had a son. And he had two big dogs, you know? The German shepherd. And when we walked, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he let the dogs go. And-- and he jump. And I said, you run. I will stay because I like dogs, and they will stay with me. And they came over. And they-- he beat me in my behind. And that's it.
